paragraphs.text: After this Abraham went to the Philistines, and out of fear said of Sarah that she was his sister; “and Abimelech king of Gerar sent and took Sarah” (Gen 20:2). But since Sarah had already undergone temptation with Pharaoh, and moreover had Isaac in her womb, and Abraham’s prayer was unceasing, no sooner had Abimelech come up to his bed than suddenly, as upon Adam, sleep fell upon him, and God said to him in a dream: “Behold, thou art a dead man, for the woman whom thou hast taken: for she is a man’s wife.” Abimelech answered: “Wilt thou slay a righteous nation? In the integrity of my heart and innocency of my hands have I done this.” Then God said: “Therefore I suffered thee not to touch her… that thou shouldest not sin against Me” (Gen 20:3–6).

Abimelech, rising early in the morning, called Abraham to him and rebuked him for nearly leading him into a grave sin. Abraham said in reply: “I was afraid, and so I called Sarah my sister; and I did not lie in this, for she is indeed my sister, the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother — my sister by the father, because she is the daughter of my father’s brother, but not my sister by the mother, because Haran, the son of Terah, did not take a sister to wife, but a foreigner, and she, loving her own kin, remained in her own tribe and did not wish to go and live with her son Lot or with her daughters Sarah and Milcah.” Then Abimelech said to Sarah: “Behold, I have given a thousand pieces of silver unto thy brother” (Gen 20:16), and I restore thee to him with gifts, because thou hast laid a covering upon the eyes of all that are with me, and openly rebuked me before all. Abimelech says, “Thou hast laid a covering upon the eyes of all who are with me” — that is, she shamed all who were with Abimelech, openly rebuking him before all. For when the sudden sleep that fell upon Abimelech showed Sarah that God was her helper, then with a loud voice in the presence of all she said to him: It does not become thee, leaving thy wife, to take another for adultery. But if Sarah after conceiving Isaac had not regained her youth, of course Abimelech would not have felt desire for one who was already ninety years old. “And Abraham prayed unto God: and God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his maidservants; and they bore children” (Gen 20:17). For from the time when Abimelech had set himself to take Sarah, up until her return to Abraham, the pains of childbirth tormented the women in Abimelech’s house, and those for whom the time of childbirth had already come could not bring forth.
